papers.title: Comparing hypervelocity star populations from the Large Magellanic Cloud and the Milky Way
papers.author_names: Fraser A. Evans Tommaso Marchetti Elena Maria Rossi Josephine F. W. Baggen Sanne Bloot

abstract.text: We predict and compare the distributions and properties of hyper-velocity stars (HVSs) ejected from the centres of the Milky Way (MW) and the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). In our model, HVSs are ejected at a constant rate -- equal in both galaxies -- via the Hills mechanism and are propagated in a combined potential, where the LMC orbits the MW on its first infall. By selecting $m>2\\\\, \\\\mathrm{M_\\\\odot}$ HVSs well-separated from the Magellanic Clouds and Galactic midplane, we identify mock HVSs which would stand out from ordinary stars in the stellar halo in future data releases from the Gaia satellite and the Vera C. Rubin Observatory's Legacy Survey of Space and Time (LSST). We find that in these deep surveys, LMC HVSs will outnumber MW ones by a factor $\\\\sim 2.5$, as HVSs can more easily escape from the shallower potential of the LMC. At an assumed HVS ejection rate of $10^{-4} \\\\, \\\\mathrm{yr^{-1}}$, HVSs detectable in the final Gaia data release and LSST from the LMC (MW) will number $125_{-12}^{+11}$ ($50_{-8}^{+7}$) and $140_{-11}^{+10}$ ($42_{-7}^{+6}$), respectively. The MW and LMC HVS populations show different kinematics and spatial distributions. While LMC HVSs have more modest total velocities and larger Galactocentric distances clustered around those of the LMC itself, HVSs from the MW show broader distributions, including a prominent high-velocity tail above $500 \\\\, \\\\mathrm{km \\\\ s^{-1}}$ that contains at least half of the stars. These predictions are robust against reasonable variation of the Galactic potential and of the LMC central black hole mass.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper models and compares hyper-velocity star (HVS) populations ejected from the Milky Way (MW) and the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) via the Hills mechanism, using a combined gravitational potential that includes the LMC's first infall orbit. It predicts that LMC HVSs will outnumber MW HVSs by a factor of ~2.5 due to the LMC's shallower gravitational potential, leading to easier ejection and greater survival in the halo. LMC HVSs exhibit lower velocities and larger Galactocentric distances clustered near the LMC, while MW HVSs show a broad kinematic distribution with a prominent high-velocity tail (>500 km/s) containing at least half of the population, a result robust to variations in the Galactic potential and central black hole mass.

source_fields.title: Comparing hypervelocity star populations from the Large Magellanic Cloud and the Milky Way

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Discussion: The experiment finds that Gaia DR4 is expected to contain only 1.5–1.4 LMC HVSs (mass >5 M⊙) and 1.6–1.4 GC HVSs (v_ej >1500 km/s, t_flight <20 Myr), with HVS3 and S5-HVS1 being rare exceptions suggesting either higher ejection rates or a top-heavy IMF in the LMC center. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel method to identify and trace hyper-velocity stars originating from the Galactic Centre using high-precision Gaia astrometry, addressing the longstanding problem of ambiguously assigning HVS origins and resolving whether these stars are ejected via the Hills mechanism or alternative disc-driven processes. Summary and Conclusion: This study predicts that future surveys like Gaia DR4 and LSST will detect significantly more hypervelocity stars from the Large Magellanic Cloud than from the Galactic Center, with distinct spatial distributions and velocity characteristics, and finds these predictions robust to uncertainties in the central black hole mass and gravitational potential. Mock Ejected Star Catalogues: This section describes a Monte Carlo model for generating realistic populations of hypervelocity stars ejected from galactic centers via the Hills mechanism, detailing the physical parameters and velocity distributions used to simulate their ejection and initial properties. HVS space and velocity distributions: This section demonstrates that HVSs ejected from the Galactic Center and the LMC have distinct spatial and velocity distributions, with LMC HVSs forming a leading arm due to their orbital boost and GC HVSs remaining close to the Galactic center, enabling potential observational distinction between the two populations. Impact of MW+LMC potential assumptions: The predicted numbers of Gaia- and LSST-detectable hypervelocity stars from the Galactic Center and the Large Magellanic Cloud are largely insensitive to variations in the Milky Way's dark matter halo mass, LMC central black hole mass, or LMC mass, though LSST detections from both sources show slight decreases with increasing halo mass. Hyper-velocity stars observable by future surveys: This section identifies and characterizes hyper-velocity stars bright enough to be detected by Gaia and LSST, highlighting that Gaia is more effective at detecting GC HVSs, especially massive ones, while LSST will be capable of observing a broader range of HVSs, particularly those in the Galactic plane.

title: Comparing hypervelocity star populations from the Large Magellanic Cloud and the Milky Way

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents theoretical predictions and simulations of HVS populations from MW and LMC, using mock HVSs for future surveys. No actual observational data or catalog of real stars is presented.

papers.title: Confirming NGC 6231 as the parent cluster of the runaway high-mass X-ray binary HD 153919/4U 1700-37 with Gaia DR2
papers.author_names: Vincent van der Meij Difeng Guo Lex Kaper Mathieu Renzo

abstract.text: A significant fraction of the most massive stars move through space with a high velocity. One of the possible physical explanations is that a supernova in a compact binary system results in a high recoil velocity of the system. If the system remains bound, it can be subsequently observed as a spectroscopic binary (SB1), a high-mass X-ray binary, a compact binary, and finally a gravitational-wave event. If such a system is traced back to its parent cluster, binary evolution models can be tested in great detail. The Gaia proper motions and parallaxes are used to demonstrate that the high-mass X-ray binary HD153919/4U 1700-37 originates from NGC6231, the nucleus of the OB association Sco OB1. The O supergiant and its compact companion, of which the physical nature (a neutron star or a black hole) is unknown, move with a space velocity of 63 km/s with respect to NGC6231. The kinematical age of the system is 2.2 Myr. The parallaxes and accurate proper motions in Gaia DR2 were used to perform a membership analysis of NGC 6231. The distance to NGC6231 is 1.63 kpc. Isochrone fitting results in an age of 4.7 Myr. With the identification of NGC6231 as the parent cluster, the upper limit on the age of the progenitor of 4U1700-37 at the moment of the supernova explosion is 3.0 Myr. With these constraints, the evolutionary history of the system can be reconstructed with an initial mass of the progenitor of the compact object >60 Msun. Given its current high space velocity and the derived evolutionary history, the compact object in the system is more likely to have received a large natal kick, which suggests that it is more likely a neutron star than a black hole. HD153919/4U1700-37 might be a prototype in the Milky Way for the progenitor of gravitational wave events such as GW190412

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: runaway-related mechanisms LLM confirmed: Explicitly studies a runaway high-mass X-ray binary with high space velocity (63 km/s), tracing its origin to parent cluster, discussing ejection mechanisms and kinematics

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: Discussion: The runaway high-mass X-ray binary HD 153919/4U 1700-37 originated from the outskirts of the NGC 6231 cluster, with a space velocity of 63 km s⁻¹, and its evolutionary history suggests a dynamical ejection scenario rather than formation at the cluster center, despite the presence of massive stars and a possible supernova event in the cluster. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework for understanding how high-mass X-ray binaries form in binary systems where mass transfer inverts the mass ratio, enabling the study of accretion physics and stellar evolution with unprecedented precision, while addressing the problem of how massive stars in binary systems evolve and contribute to galactic chemical enrichment and gravitational-wave source formation. Membership NGC 6231: The membership of NGC 6231 is determined through a manual astrometric selection from Gaia DR2 data, followed by astrometric corrections and a membership analysis to distinguish cluster members from field stars. The age of NGC 6231: The age of NGC 6231 is determined through isochrone fitting to subpopulations (full, pre-main-sequence, and main-sequence) using PARSEC and MIST models, with extinction and metallicity parameters carefully accounted for to derive a consistent age of approximately 10–15 million years. Appendix C Isochrone parameters: This section outlines the stellar isochrone parameters and modeling settings for both PARSEC and MIST, including adopted physical assumptions, photometric systems, and initial conditions. The kinematical age of the runaway system: This section reconstructs the kinematical path of the runaway high-mass X-ray binary HD 153919/4U 1700-37 and its parent association NGC 6231 using Gaia DR2 proper motions and radial velocities from prior studies to determine its kinematical age and thus the time of supernova explosion. Appendix A ADQL selection candidate members NGC 6231: This section queries Gaia DR2 data to select candidate members of the NGC 6231 cluster based on positional, astrometric, photometric, and radial velocity criteria within a specific galactic longitude and latitude range. Updating the system parameters of HD 153919 / 4U 1700-37: Reassessing the system parameters of HD 153919/4U 1700-37 with a revised Gaia EDR3 distance and updated mass determinations from eccentric orbit modeling yields consistent values for the O supergiant and compact object masses, inclination, and luminosity. Appendix B Membership probability thresholds for NGC 6231: Higher membership probability thresholds in NGC 6231 result in fewer candidate members, with no candidates remaining at a threshold of 0.95.

title: Confirming NGC 6231 as the parent cluster of the runaway high-mass X-ray binary HD 153919/4U 1700-37

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper uses Gaia DR2 astrometry (proper motions, parallaxes) to trace origin of specific runaway high-mass X-ray binary HD153919/4U1700-37 to parent cluster NGC6231. Presents observational data (space velocity, kinematical age) for this specific stellar object.
